flashcards.Back: For suspected cardiac origin, clinicians must apply defibrillation pads early due to the high risk of VF or VT.
According to Maine protocols, resuscitation may be terminated after 20 minutes of persistent asystole for ALS clinicians.
Maine protocols allow termination of resuscitation after 60 minutes of persistent Ventricular Fibrillation or Pulseless VT.
Resuscitation for slow and/or wide complex Pulseless Electrical Activity (PEA) may be terminated after 20 minutes.
Maine protocols require 45 minutes of fast/narrow PEA before considering the termination of resuscitation efforts.
Suspected cardiac chest pain patients must be assessed for STEMI criteria to ensure rapid transport to a specialty center.
Manage patients at Red 32 protocol by maintaining perfusion while avoiding fluid overload that could worsen pulmonary edema.
Receives deoxygenated blood from the superior and inferior vena cavae before passing through the tricuspid valve.
The thickest chamber that pumps oxygenated blood through the aortic valve into the systemic circulation.
The Sinoatrial (SA) node, located in the right atrium, which normally initiates the heart's electrical impulse.
Briefly delays the electrical impulse to allow the ventricles to fill with blood before contraction.
Calculated as $CO = SV \times HR$, where $SV$ is stroke volume and $HR$ is heart rate.
Temporary chest pain caused by reduced blood flow to the heart muscle, usually relieved by rest or nitroglycerin.
The death of heart muscle tissue due to a complete blockage of a coronary artery.
Administer 162-324 mg of chewed baby aspirin to prevent further platelet aggregation in suspected ACS.
Assists in vasodilation to reduce preload and cardiac workload; requires SBP > 100 mmHg and no ED meds.
Backup of blood into the lungs leading to pulmonary edema, rales, and shortness of breath.
Backup of blood into the body, causing Jugular Venous Distension (JVD) and peripheral edema.
A chaotic, disorganized heart rhythm that produces no pulse and is a primary shockable rhythm for an AED.
A rapid heart rhythm originating in the ventricles; shockable by AED if the patient is pulseless.
A consistent rate of 100 to 120 compressions per minute for all age groups.
Compressions must be at least 2 inches (5 cm) but no more than 2.4 inches (6 cm) deep.
30 compressions to 2 ventilations for both single and two-rescuer adult CPR.
Changes from 30:2 (single rescuer) to 15:2 (two rescuers) for children and infants.
Early Access, Early CPR, Early Defibrillation, Early ALS, and Post-Cardiac Arrest Care.
Immediately resume chest compressions for 2 minutes before the AED re-analyzes the rhythm.
The restoration of a palpable pulse and blood pressure following cardiac arrest management.
The unique ability of cardiac muscle cells to generate their own electrical impulses independently.
The largest artery in the body that carries oxygenated blood from the left ventricle to the systemic circulation.
Vessels that supply oxygen-rich blood directly to the heart muscle (myocardium).
The only veins in the human body that carry oxygenated blood (from the lungs back to the left atrium).
The only arteries in the human body that carry deoxygenated blood (from the heart to the lungs).
The amount of blood returning to the ventricles at the end of diastole, stretching the muscle fibers.
The resistance the left ventricle must overcome to eject blood into the systemic circulation.
Beck's Triad: JVD, muffled heart sounds, and narrowing pulse pressure (obstructive shock).
Classically described as a sudden, tearing or ripping sensation radiating between the shoulder blades.
Position the patient upright, provide high-flow oxygen, and consider CPAP if severe respiratory distress exists.
Severe headache, blurred vision, and altered mental status with blood pressure usually > 180/120 mmHg.
The brachial pulse is the primary site for assessing circulation in an unresponsive infant.
The carotid or femoral pulse is used to assess circulation in an unresponsive child.
Standard placement is upper right chest and lower left chest (mid-axillary line below the nipple).
Manual left uterine displacement must be performed during chest compressions to improve venous return.
A condition characterized by the thickening and hardening of the arterial walls, reducing elasticity.
The buildup of fatty plaques (cholesterol) within the arterial walls, a major cause of ACS.
A deficiency of oxygenated blood reaching a tissue, often causing reversible cell injury and pain.
Permanent death of tissue caused by a prolonged lack of blood supply and oxygen.
The heart valve located between the right atrium and the right ventricle.
The heart valve located between the left atrium and the left ventricle.
The time during which the heart muscle cell cannot be restimulated to contract again.
To deliver a controlled electric shock to stop disorganized rhythms and allow the SA node to restart correctly.
